title: Baby Changing Table Topper
summary: A shallow open box that sits on a dresser to turn it into a changing station, with a divider for wipes. An hour with a 1x4 and a Kreg jig.
description:
This is the cheapest way to get a changing station: instead of buying a dedicated changing table you'll use for a year, build a shallow tray that sits on top of a dresser you already own and holds a changing pad in place. When you're done with diapers, the topper comes off and the dresser goes back to being a dresser.

The whole thing is a four-sided box from a single 1x4, with a divider positioned to create a cubby for wipes. Because it's built to hold a specific changing pad, the one real instruction is to measure your pad first and size the box to it — the dimensions here are a starting point, not gospel.

The only thing that matters for safety is that the topper can't slide off the dresser with a baby on it. Fasten it down — a few pocket screws down into the dresser top, or an L-bracket at the back — so it stays put. A topper that slides is worse than no topper.

tools.note: Joins the box and fastens it to the dresser with hidden pocket holes.
Or a miter saw. Square crosscuts on the 1x4.

materials.name: Non-toxic finish
materials.note: It's a baby item — use a finish rated safe once cured.

cutList.note: Size to your dresser top and changing pad.
Two ends plus a divider; position the divider to fit your changing pad and leave a wipes cubby.

steps.title: Measure the pad, then cut the box
steps.body: Measure your changing pad and your dresser top before any of these cuts; the listed sizes are a starting point, not gospel, and the topper only works if the pad drops into it. As listed, cut two front and back pieces 38 1/2" and three ends-and-divider pieces 15 1/2" from the 1x4, all at the board's full 3 1/2" width. Check the board first: those five pieces total 123 1/2" and a nominal 10 ft 1x4 is 120", so one board will not yield the list as written — buy a longer board, a second one, or shorten the box to your own dresser. With the two ends set between the front and back, the finished tray comes out 38 1/2" long by 17" deep.

steps.title: Measure your pad, then build the box
steps.body: With the pad measured and the parts cut, drill two 3/4" pocket holes in each end of the two 15 1/2" end pieces, on the faces that will end up inside the box where they are hidden. Set the ends between the 38 1/2" front and back, square them with the speed square, and join with 1-1/4" pocket hole screws and glue. Measure both diagonals and correct until they match before the glue grabs — a racked tray will not hold a pad square and will not sit flat on the dresser.

steps.title: Add the divider
steps.body: Slide the third 15 1/2" piece in as the divider. Two 3/4" ends and a 3/4" divider leave 36 1/4" of interior in a 38 1/2" box, so set the actual pad in, push it tight to one end, and mark both faces of the divider against it — whatever is left over becomes the cubby, and it lands right where a wipes container naturally goes. Drill pocket holes in the divider's ends before it goes in, then fasten it through the front and back with 1-1/4" pocket hole screws and glue, checking it is square to the box on both faces.

steps.title: Secure it to the dresser and finish
steps.body: The topper must not be able to slide off with a baby on it. Fasten it down — a few pocket holes drilled through the back piece into the dresser top, or an L-bracket at the back — so it stays put; a topper that slides is worse than no topper. Then sand every surface and round the top edges thoroughly, because that rail is what a baby's arm will be against. Finish with a non-toxic product rated safe once cured, coat the inside as well as the outside, and let it cure fully before the pad and the baby go in.

images.alt: A shallow wooden changing-table topper sitting on a dresser with a changing pad
